food_subgroup: Chinese rice bowl set meal with protein, vegetables, and rice

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for a Chinese-Singaporean value set rice bowl meal. FOODON_00002473 (rice dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FHR (rice-based composite dish) used as closest parent. No exact code for a Singaporean Chinese value set rice bowl.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept identified for this composite set meal.

main_ingredients: protein (chicken, pork, or tofu depending on selection)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 450 g, comprising a portion of steamed white rice (approximately 200 g cooked), a protein component (approximately 150 g), and a vegetable side (approximately 100 g), as typically served in a Singaporean Chinese rice kitchen set meal.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Database — Steamed white rice, Chinese braised/stir-fried chicken with vegetables, composite meal estimation
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry exists for 'Value Set C' from First Bowl Rice Kitchen. Nutrient values are estimated by component-weighted averaging using HPB Singapore Foodo entries for: (1) steamed white rice per 100 g, (2) Chinese braised chicken per 100 g, (3) stir-fried leafy vegetables per 100 g. Proportions assumed: 44% rice, 33% protein, 22% vegetables. Values represent a plausible mid-range for a standard Chinese rice bowl set meal in Singapore. USDA SR Legacy used as secondary cross-check for individual components.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (steamed) and mixed rice-based meal analogues
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 'Value Set C' as a composite restaurant meal. GI estimated at 65 based on the dominant carbohydrate component being steamed white rice, for which the University of Sydney GI Database reports a GI of approximately 64–72 (mean ~68) for Asian-style steamed white rice. The presence of protein, fat, and vegetables in the set meal is expected to moderately lower the effective GI of the composite meal; a conservative estimate of 65 is applied. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (65 × 87.75 g carbohydrate in 450 g serving) / 100 = 57.0, rounded to 57.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 320 mg per 100 g; a full 450 g serving delivers approximately 1440 mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a single serving of this set meal may contribute approximately 60–70% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced-sauce or no-added-salt preparation is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High carbohydrate load from steamed white rice (GI ~65, GL ~57 per serving) with moderate protein and fat buffering
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size carefully, as the steamed white rice component contributes a medium-GI carbohydrate load; substituting brown rice or reducing the rice portion while increasing the protein and vegetable components can help attenuate postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~1.2 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium (~320 mg per 100 g) from sauce and seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption frequency of this set meal due to its combined sodium and saturated fat load; choosing steamed or lightly seasoned protein preparations and requesting sauce on the side can reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Value Set C Rice Bowl is a Singaporean Chinese set meal served at First Bowl Rice Kitchen (Funan outlet) and similar Chinese rice kitchen restaurants in Singapore. The meal typically comprises a portion of steamed white rice accompanied by a protein component — commonly braised or stir-fried chicken, pork, or tofu — and a side of blanched or stir-fried leafy vegetables, all finished with a savoury sauce or gravy. The rice is soft and slightly sticky, the protein is tender and well-seasoned with soy-based or oyster-sauce-based marinades, and the vegetables provide a mild, fresh counterpoint. This format is a staple of Singapore's Chinese economic rice (cai fan) tradition, offering a balanced, affordable, and filling meal. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy, carbohydrate-dominant meal with meaningful protein and low-to-moderate fat. The sodium content is elevated due to soy sauce and braising liquids. The glycemic index is medium (~65), driven primarily by the steamed white rice component, though the protein and fat co-ingestion moderates the glycemic response. Clinically, individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease should monitor portion size and request reduced-sauce preparations. The meal is dairy-free and suitable for most dietary patterns, though it is not certified halal or vegetarian by default.

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry exists for 'Value Set C' from First Bowl Rice Kitchen in any approved primary source. Nutrient values are estimated by component-weighted averaging of HPB Singapore Foodo entries for steamed white rice, Chinese braised chicken, and stir-fried vegetables. The GI is an analogous estimate based on steamed white rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium because the component-level sources are approved and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible, but the exact dish composition is not independently verified.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo component entries used for this estimation. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo component entries used for this estimation. 3. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo component entries used for this estimation. 4. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in steamed rice and lightly cooked Chinese dishes per HPB and USDA SR Legacy component data. 5. The food name 'Value Set C' is a menu label; the canonical English name used here is 'Value Set C Rice Bowl' to reflect the actual food format. 6. Serving size of 450 g is an estimate based on typical Singaporean Chinese rice bowl set meal portions; actual restaurant portion may vary. 7. Halal status is not confirmed; First Bowl Rice Kitchen is not listed as a MUIS-certified halal establishment as of the processing date. 8. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this composite meal exists in the University of Sydney GI Database.
